pages.text: 50 mm wide, and 50 mm high. The specimen was supported at two ends, between which it has a span length of 150 mm. The MTS was used to apply a half-sinusoidal loading with a frequency of 10 Hz at the midpoint of the specimen. The stress ratio S is deﬁned as follows: S ¼ rm RB ð3Þ where rm is the ultimate ﬂexural stress (MPa), and RB is the ﬂexural strength (MPa) determined by the ﬂexural test discussed previ- ously. In this experiment ﬁve stress ratios ranged (0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4 and 0.5) were used for each test. The loading cycles at material fail- ure, was recorded as the fatigue life. Four identical specimens were used for each test of each mixture, and the average value of test re- sults is obtained. 4.5. Freezing–thaw cycling test At low temperature when water in the voids of AC material is changing to ices, AC mixture is subjected to ice’s expanding force; while at warmer temperature when ices thaws AC’s structure be- comes loose. In this study the laboratory test was performed to simulate the freezing–thaw cycling effects following the speciﬁca- tion [20]. Eight identical cylindrical specimens measuring 101.6 mm (diameter) by 63.5 mm (height) were prepared and di- vided into two groups with four specimens for each group. One group of specimens was cured under water at 25 /C176C for 20 min; then each specimen was sealed in a plastic package ﬁlled with 10 ml water and then vacuumed under a pressure of 98.3– 98.7 kPa. The sealed and vacuumed specimen with water was stored in a refrigerator for 16 h at /C0 18 /C176C. Subsequently, these specimens were bathed in the warm water at 60 /C176C for 2 h. Then, these four frozen–thawed samples and another group of four ‘‘fresh” samples were both bathed in water at 25 /C176C for more than 2 h to achieve a uniform temperature. Afterwards, split indirect tensile strength tests were performed on both the fresh and fro- zen–thawed specimens, following the speciﬁcation AASHTO T283 as shown in Fig. 3 . The split indirect tensile strength (SITS, MPa) was determined as follows [20]: RT ¼ 0:00628 F h ð4Þ where F is the ultimate loading at failure (kN), h is the sample height, and 0.006287 is a coefﬁcient. The SITS ratio before and after freezing–thaw (TSR) is calculated as follows: TSR ¼ RT2 RT1 /C2 100% ð5Þ where RT1 is the SITS of the fresh specimen, RT2 is the SITS of the fro- zen–thawed specimen. The fracture toughness (area under the stress–strain curve) can be determined from the area under the measured stress–strain Fig. 2. Rutting test. Fig. 3. Split indirect tensile strength test. Performance of ﬁber reinforced asphalt concrete under environmental temperature and water effects Qinwu Xu a,*, Huaxin Chen b, Jorge A. Prozzi a a Civil, Architectural and Environmental Engineering, The University of Texas at Austin, TX 78712, USA b School of Materials Science and Engineering, Chang’an University, Xi’an 710064, China article info Article history: Received 2 September 2009 Accepted 28 March 2010 Available online 18 April 2010 Keywords: Asphalt concrete Fabrics Mechanical property Temperature Water effect abstract This paper studies the reinforcing effects and mechanisms of ﬁbers for asphalt concrete (AC) mixtures under the environment temperature and water effects. Four typical ﬁber types – polyester, polyacryloni- trile, lignin and asbestos – are studied. Laboratory tests were conduct on the ﬁber reinforced AC (FRAC) to measure its strength, strain and fatigue behavior. Results show that ﬁbers have signiﬁcantly improved AC’s rutting resistance, fatigue life, and toughness. The ﬂexural strength and ultimate ﬂexural strain, and the split indirect tensile strength (SITS) at low temperature have also improved. The polymer ﬁbers (polyester and polyacrylonitrile) have improved rutting resistance, fatigue life, and SITS more signiﬁ- cantly than lignin and asbestos ﬁbers, which may be primarily due to their greater networking function; while lignin and asbestos ﬁbers result in greater ﬂexural strength and ultimate ﬂexural strain, which may be primarily due to their greater asphalt stabilization effect. However, ﬁber’s effect under the water freez- ing–thaw effect does not seem promising, and the SITS of FRAC with lignin and asbestos ﬁbers even reduces to some extent under this effect. It is also found that a ﬁber content of 0.35% by mass of mixture achieves the optimum performance outputs of rutting resistance and SITS for polyester ﬁber. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Asphalt concrete (AC) is a composite material consisting of aggregate, asphalt binder (mastic), and air void. AC has been pri- marily used as a material in constructing road and airport pave- ments. However, under the effects of repeated vehicle loading at high temperature, moisture cycling, and low-temperature contrac- tion, AC mixture is susceptible to distresses of rutting (permanent deformation), stripping (separation of asphalts from aggregates), and cracking, etc. Accordingly, additives have been used to alter the engineering properties (e.g. strength) of asphalt binder and im- prove the performance of AC material for pavement use. These additives primarily include the organic polymers as illustrated in numerous literatures [1]. In comparison, not many ﬁbers have been used in AC materials due to their high cost and limited re- search demonstrations. However, previous researches have identi- ﬁed ﬁber’s effects on improving the engineering properties and performances of asphalt matrix. Fiber prevents asphalt leakage due to its function of asphalt absorption [2,3]. It increases the Mar- shall stability, reduces the voids in mineral aggregates (VMA) to improve adhesions between aggregates [4]. Fiber increases the vis- cosity and stiffness of asphalts [5] also improves AC’s moisture sus- ceptibility [6], wearing resistance [7], creep compliance and rutting resistance [8]. It reduces AC’s reﬂective cracking [9,10], increases the low-temperature anti-cracking property, fatigue life [11–13], and durability [1,14,15]. It was also noted

reinforcing effects and mechanisms of these ﬁbers are compared and analyzed. 3. Materials 3.1. Asphalt The asphalt type of AH-90 produced by the Panjin Petrochemical Industry was used for all AC samples and experiments. 3.2. Fibers Four typical ﬁber types were investigated, including two polymer ﬁbers: one polyester (BoniFiber@) produced by the Kapejo Inc., and one polyacrylonitrile (Dolanit@AC) produced by the Kelheim Fibers Inc., one lignin ﬁber produced by the Beijing Pan China Municipal Tec. CO., and one asbestos ﬁber produced by the Sichuan Yitong Asbestos Products Inc. Fig. 1 displays these ﬁbers and their micro- structures observed using the Scanning Electronic Microscopic (SEM) device. The dimensions and basic physical properties of these ﬁbers are presented in Table 1 (provided by the manufacturers). 3.3. AC mixtures AC mixtures were designed using the Marshall design method, one of the most popular AC mixture design methods. The aggregate gradation, optimum asphalt content, and air void are summarized in Table 2 . The controlled AC mixture uses a ﬁber content of 0.3% by mass of AC mixture according to the manufacturer recom- mendation. Other researchers have reported that the optimum ﬁber content for the organic and mineral ﬁbers could be 0.3% and 0.4%, respectively, based on the perfor- mance tests [16]. In order to capture the inﬂuences of ﬁber content, different ﬁber contents—0.00%, 0.20%, 0.35%, and 0.50% (by mass of mixture)—were used for the AC mixtures with polyester ﬁber. 4. Experimental program 4.1. ESM test Fiber’s microstructure characteristics are observed using the SEM–EPMA (S-570) equipment manufactured by the Hitachi Group, in order to help understand ﬁber’s reinforcing mechanism. Their microstructures are presented in Fig. 1 , and will be further discussed later. 4.2. High temperature rutting test In this research a 1/4-scaled accelerated loading test was per- formed on AC mixtures to measure the developed rutting depths (permanent deformations of AC mixtures) at 60 /C176C (representing the high temperature of AC material in the Summer weather), fol- lowing the speciﬁcation [20]. Before the test, the loose AC mixtures were compacted in a steel mold under a steel roller, resulting in a solid slab measuring 30 cm (length) /C2 30 cm (width) /C2 5c m (height) with the target air voids (see Table 2 ). Subsequently, in the test a single wheel with a standard vehicle tire pressure of 0.7 MPa was applied on the surface of the AC slab as shown in Fig. 2 . The wheel was rolled on the AC slab within a distance of 23 cm with a speed of 42 cycle/min (16.1 cm/s) along one direc- tion. The developed rutting depths were measured at an interval 5 min. 4.3. Low-temperature ﬂexural test Under the effects of low temperatures, thermal cracking may occur in AC mixtures [21]. Thus, the AC mixture with greater ten- sile strength and ultimate strain at low temperature can more effectively resist the cracking occurrence and sustain cracking propagation before material fails [22]. In this research the ﬂexural strength and ultimate ﬂexural strain of AC mixtures are measured at 0 /C176C and/C0 10 /C176C, respectively, following the speciﬁcation[20,23]. The laboratory-compacted AC slab was cut into beam speci- mens measuring 30 mm (width) by 35 mm (height) by 250 mm (length). The third-point ﬂexural test following the speciﬁcation [23] was conducted on the beam specimen with a span length of 200 mm. The Material Testing System (MTS), a clos

lignin and asbestos ﬁbers have many interweaved branches with non-uniform sizes, and the lignin ﬁber has porous structures and rough surface textures, which would improve their speciﬁc surface areas and the capability to absorb and hold asphalt binder together. 5.3. Fatigue life The third-point fatigue test results summarized in Table 3 indi- cate that ﬁbers have pronouncedly improved the fatigue life of AC mixtures. E.g. at the stress ratio of 0.5 fatigue life at material failure when specimen broke has increased by 57.66%, 66.78%, 40.88%, and 22.52% for the AC with the polyester, polyacrylonitrile, asbes- tos, and lignin ﬁber, respectively. The improvement of fatigue life could be attributed to ﬁber’s absorption and adhesion of asphalt, networking function, bridging of cracking, and reallocation of stresses as discussed previously. Generally the material’s fatigue life, N f, can be expressed as a function of stress magnitude [28] as follows: Nf ¼ ArB m or log ðNf Þ¼ k1 /C0 n log rm ð6Þ where K1 = log(A) and n = /C0 B, are two material-dependent parameters. However, these ﬁve AC mixtures (one ordinary mixture and four FRACs) have different asphalt contents, air voids, and ﬁber types, although they have the same aggregate gradation and ﬁber con- tent. Therefore, it may be more reasonable to express the fatigue life as a function of stress ratio (see Eq. (3)) rather than stress mag- nitude, in order to account for the effects of mixture design on ﬂex- ural strength. The model for fatigue life and stress ratio is re- derived as follows: logðNf Þ¼ k2 /C0 n log S ð7Þ where S is stress ratio (see Eq. (3)). logðNf Þ¼ k2 /C0 n½log rm /C0 log fr /C138¼½ k2 þ n logðfr Þ/C138 /C0 n log rm ð8Þ Comparing (7) and (8) : k2 ¼ k1 /C0 n logðRBÞð 9Þ where RB is ﬂexural strength, k2 and n are two material-dependent parameters. The measured values of fatigue life with stress ratios are plotted in Fig. 8. They show to have a linear relationship follow- ing Eq. (6), with model parameters presented in Table 3. The fatigue lives of these AC mixtures could be ranked in an increased order as follows: no ﬁber, lignin, asbestos, polyester, and polyacrylonitril ﬁ- ber. The model parameter n can be used as an index to indicate the sensitivity of fatigue life to stress ratio. Result shows that the AC mixture with asbestos ﬁber has the greatest sensitivity (greatest n value), then the polyester, polyacrylonitrile, no ﬁber, and lignin. 5.4. Split indirect tensile strengths Test results show that ﬁbers have improved the SITS of AC mix- tures, as depicted in Fig. 9 (see RT1 in this ﬁgure). E.g. polyester, polyacrylonitrile, lignin and asbestos ﬁbers have improved SITS by 6.88%, 8.30%, 1.11% (very small though), and 3.74% (very small though), respectively. The improvement of SITS would be attrib- uted to ﬁber’s absorption and adhesion of asphalts that improves the interface adhesion strength [19], and ﬁber’s networking and bridging cracking effects as discussed previously. Result also shows that the AC mixtures with polyester and polyacrylonitrile ﬁbers have higher SITS values than that with asbestos and lignin ﬁbers, which would be due to their higher networking effects discussed previously. The polyacrylonitrile ﬁber has the highest SITS, which could be due to its highest length/diameter ratio and tensile strength to improve its networking effect. 5.5. Toughness The stress–strain curve under the indirect split tensile test is plotted in Fig. 10 (fresh specimens), from w

can be calculated as the area under the curve. Results summarized in Table 4 show that both the pre-cracking and post-cracking toughness of FRAC have increased. E.g. the polyester, polyacryloni- trile, lignin and asbestos ﬁbers have signiﬁcantly improved the toughness especially the post-crack toughness (i.e. improves 41.54%, 71.01%, 15.47%, and 26.67%, respectively). Fiber poses greater modulus and elongation than asphalt, performing as bridges to resist cracking propagation and material failure, and sustain greater stress and strain before material failure, resulting in the improved toughness. 5.6. Water freezing–thaw results Results show that the polyester and polyacrylonitrile ﬁbers have improved the SITS of frozen–thawed AC mixtures to some ex- tent (see R T2 in Fig. 9 ), e.g. by 4.89% and 3.87% though not signiﬁ- cant. However, it is noted that the SITS of the frozen–thawed AC mixtures with lignin and asbesotos ﬁbers have reduced than the ordinary AC mixtures (see RT2 in Fig. 9), which could be due to their relatively large air voids after adding ﬁbers (see Table 2 ). The in- creased air voids provide more spaces to contain water and form ices at low temperatures, resulting in greater expanding forces of ices. It is also found that the split indirect tensile strength ratio be- fore and after freezing–thawing effect (TSR) of FRAC are lower than that of the ordinary AC mixture, as shown in Fig. 11 . The reason could be primarily due to the increase of air voids as discussed above. In addition, different thermal contraction rates of ﬁbers and asphalts may result in interface sliding between aggregates and ﬁbers, which further produces the void spaces inside AC mix- ture. Therefore, a greater compaction effort or a slightly higher compaction temperature (equal viscosity temperature) than the ordinary AC mixture maybe used to reduce the level of air voids and then improve the freezing–thaw resistance. 5.7. Inﬂuence of ﬁber content In order to account for the inﬂuences of ﬁber content on the material properties, different ﬁber contents, 0%, 0.20%, 0.35%, and 0.50% by mass of AC mixture, are used for the FRAC with polyester ﬁber. It should be noted in this study only part of the performance tests discussed in this paper – rutting depth and SITS tests – were performed due to the limited funding. 0.00 0.20 0.40 0.60 0.80 1.00 1.20 0.000 0.005 0.010 0.015 0.020 0.025 0.030 Stress (MPa) Strain No fiber Lignin fiber Asbestos fiber Polyester fiber Polyacrylonitrile fiber Fig. 10. 5.7.1. Rutting depth vs. ﬁber content Fig. 12 shows that the rutting depth decreases ﬁrst and then in- creases with increasing ﬁber content. An optimum value of ﬁber content seems to be around 0.35% that results in the lowest rutting depth. The reason is that with high ﬁber contents, excessive ﬁbers will coagulate together to form weak points in the AC mixture and thus reduce the interlock effects between aggregates for rutting resistance. 5.7.2. Split indirect tensile strength vs. ﬁber content Results summarized in Table 5 indicate that the SITS of RT1 (for fresh mixture) and RT2 (for mixtures after freezing–thaw effect) both increase ﬁrst and then decrease with increasing ﬁber content. Increasing ﬁber content reinforces AC mixtures and improves strengths. However, the air void also increases after increasing ﬁ- ber content [4] (see Table 2 ), and therefore more ices will be formed at low temperature in these air voids, resulting in higher expanding force. 6. Conclusion and recommendation This investigation has comprehensively studied the reinforcing effects and mechanisms of four typical ﬁber types for use in AC mixtures under various environmental temperatures and water freezing–thaw effect. The main conclusions are summarized as follows: /C15 Fiber has signiﬁcantly improved the rutting resistance, fatigue life, and toughness of AC mixtures. /C15 Fiber has improved the low-temperature ﬂexural strength and ultimate ﬂexural strain, and split indirect tensile strength. /C15 After the water freezing–thaw effect, the split indirect tensile strength of FRAC with polyester and polyacrylinit- rile ﬁbers has only slightly increased, but it has reduced to some extent for the lignin and asbestos ﬁbers; while the tensile strength ratios for FRAC with all these ﬁbers have reduced, one of the important reasons could be due to the increased air voids for more ices to be formed at low temperature. /C15 The polymer ﬁbers (polyester and polyacrylonitrile) have greater effects on the rutting resistance, fatigue life and split indirect tensile strength than the lignin and asbestos ﬁbers, and polyacrylonitrile ﬁber has the greatest effect, which could be primarily due to their greater networking effects dependent on ﬁber’s geometry and physical properties. /C15 The lignin and asbestos ﬁbers have greater effects on the ﬂexural strength and ultimate ﬂexural strain than the polymer ﬁbers. This could be due to their greater speciﬁc surface areas for greater asphalt absorption and stabiliza- tion as measured by previous research. /C15 A ﬁber content of 0.35% by mass by AC mixture results in the optimum performance outputs of rutting resistance and split indirect tensile strength. However, the effects of ﬁbers under the water freezing–thaw ef- fect do not seem promising, and future research including other experimental test methods (e.g. to measure the ﬁber-asphalt- aggregate interface bonding strength under the water effect), are needed to further investigate this property. Meanwhile, a greater compaction effort and/or a slightly higher compaction temperature are recommended to reduce the air voids of FRAC for optimizing ﬁ- ber’s reinforcing effects. References [1] King G et al. Additives in asphalt. J Assoc Asphalt Paving Technol 1999;68:32–69. [2] Serfass JP, Samanos J. Fiber–modiﬁed asphalt concrete characteristics, applications and behavior. J Assoc Asphalt Paving Technol 1996;64: 193–230. [3] Hassan HF, Oraimi SA, Taha R. Evaluation of open–graded fricti

Accordingly, this investigation studies ﬁber’s reinforcing effects and mechanisms on AC materials under various environmental conditions through experimental study. FRAC’s performances at high, intermediate, and low temperatures are evaluated, using four typical ﬁber types (polyester, polyacrylonitrile, asbestos, and lig- nin). The effect of water freezing and thawing is also studied. The Fig. 1. Fibers and their microstructures. 2004 Q. Xu et al. / Construction and Building Materials 24 (2010) 2003–2010

curve, as shown in Fig. 4 . The calculated toughness includes two parts: pre-crack toughness and post-crack toughness (see Fig. 4 ). 5. Analysis results and discussion 5.1. Rutting Test results in Fig. 5 show that ﬁbers have signiﬁcantly reduced AC’s rutting depths (RD). E.g., polyester, polyacrylonitrile, lignin, and asbestos ﬁbers have reduced RD by 19.57%, 32.56%, 8.43%, and 11.40% at 2500 cycles respectively. Fig. 5 also shows that the RD has a rapid increase at the early stage (primary or ﬁrst stage), which is primarily induced by the condensing of ‘‘loose” AC mixtures under the vertical pressure of vehicle loading. Afterwards, the increase rate of the RD decreases and follows a linear relationship with time, which is primarily due to the shear ﬂow of AC mixture under the shear force effect. This stage is known as the secondary stage of rutting developments for AC mixtures [22]. The slope of this linear line represents the increasing rate of RD and material stability: a higher slope value indicates a lower stability of material. Analysis results show that the measured material stability could be ranked in an increased or- der as follows: non-ﬁber (ordinary mixture), lignin, asbestos, poly- ester, and polyacrylonitrile ﬁbers. The reduction of FRAC’ rutting depth can be contributed to at least two reasons: (1) ﬁber can stabilize and hold the sticky as- phalts on its surface to resist asphalts’ ﬂowing at high tempera- tures [19] and (2) ﬁber forms a three-dimensional network in the asphalt matrix [16,19], which would reinforce the skeleton struc- ture to resist the shear force and reduce the ﬂuidity. Results also show that the polyester and polyacrylonitrile ﬁbers have much greater effects on reducing rutting depth than the lig- nin and asbestos ﬁbers, which could be due to their greater net- working function dependent on ﬁber’s geometry and physical properties. Generally the ﬁber with greater tensile strength can more effectively resist cracking, and with greater length/diameter ratio can more effectively interweave together to form a network- ing system [24]. The polyacrylonitrile ﬁber has the greatest length/ diameter ratio and tensile strength (see Table 1 ), which helps im- prove networking effect. 5.2. Flexural strength and strain Fig. 6 shows that ﬁbers have improved the mixtures’ ﬂexural strengths R B at both 0 /C176C and/C0 10 /C176C. E.g polyester, polyacrylotrile, lignin, and asbestos have improved RB by 8.16%, 6.49%, 11.77%, and 12.67% at /C0 10 /C176C, and 5.26%, 3.28%, 12.43%, and 6.08% at 0 /C176C, respectively. It also shows that all AC samples measured at 0 /C176C have greater ﬂexural strengths than that measured at /C0 10 /C176C, which is due to that the AC mixture at lower temperature is more brittle and susceptible to material failure. Fig. 7 shows that the ultimate ﬂexural strain eB of FRAC has also improved, e.g., polyes- ter, polyacrylotrile, lignin, and asbestos have improved eB by 4.00%, 2.00%, 6.00%, and 3.00% at /C0 10 /C176C, and 3.81%, 5.24%, 4.76%, and 2.62% at 0 /C176C, respectively. The reasons for these results are explained as follows. The inter- faces among aggregates are relatively weak areas where fracture occurs under ﬂexural loading, and thus the interface strength at low temperature plays a key roll in resisting cracking development and propagation. Asphalt consists of asphaltenes, resins, aromatic hydrocarbons, and saturates (parafﬁns and naphthenes) [25,26]. The saturate, a component with smal
